shortDescription.text: Zip Slip: archive extraction without member path validation

fullDescription.text: The endpoint calls zipfile.ZipFile.extractall() on an uploaded archive without validating that member filenames do not contain path traversal sequences (e.g. '../../etc/cron.d/backdoor'). An attacker can craft a malicious archive whose entries escape the extract directory and overwrite arbitrary files on the server, achieving remote code execution or denial of service.

shortDescription.text: Path traversal via unsanitized upload filename

fullDescription.text: The file upload endpoint uses the client-supplied filename directly in os.path.join() without sanitizing path traversal characters. An attacker can supply a filename like '../../etc/passwd' to write files outside the intended upload directory.

shortDescription.text: File upload with no type or size validation

fullDescription.text: The upload endpoint accepts any file type without validating the content type, file extension, or file size. An attacker can upload executable files (web shells, scripts) that may be served or executed by the server.

shortDescription.text: Server-side file paths exposed in API response

fullDescription.text: The response body includes the full server-side file path (extract_dir, file_path), leaking internal directory structure to the client. This aids further exploitation such as path traversal and local file inclusion attacks.

shortDescription.text: Temporary directory created but never cleaned up

fullDescription.text: tempfile.mkdtemp() creates a temporary directory that is never removed, even on success. Repeated requests cause disk exhaustion (denial of service). Additionally, extracted files persist with potentially sensitive content.

message.text: zipfile.ZipFile.extractall() is called on a user-uploaded archive without validating that archive member paths do not contain directory traversal sequences. A crafted zip with entries like '../../../../tmp/cron.d/malicious' will write files outside the intended extract_dir, enabling arbitrary file overwrite and potential remote code execution.

properties.remediation: Iterate over zip_ref.namelist(), validate each entry with os.path.realpath() to ensure it resolves within extract_dir, then extract individually. Alternatively use zip_ref.extract() per member after validation.

message.text: The upload endpoint at POST /api/file/upload uses the user-supplied file.filename directly in os.path.join(upload_dir, file.filename) without sanitization. An attacker can supply '../../etc/crontab' as the filename to write files to arbitrary locations outside /tmp/uploads.

properties.remediation: Use werkzeug.utils.secure_filename() on the uploaded filename, or generate a random filename and store the original name in metadata.

message.text: POST /api/file/upload accepts any file without validating file extension, MIME type, or file size. An attacker can upload web shells (.py, .php), executables, or oversized files to cause code execution or denial of service.

properties.remediation: Validate file extension against an allowlist, check Content-Type header, enforce a maximum file size via Flask's MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH, and store uploads outside the webroot.

message.text: Both /api/file/upload and /api/file/extract return full server-side file paths in their JSON responses ('path', 'extracted_files', 'extract_dir'). This discloses internal directory structure to unauthenticated callers, aiding further path traversal or LFI attacks.

properties.remediation: Return only relative filenames or opaque identifiers. Never expose absolute server paths in API responses.

message.text: tempfile.mkdtemp() at POST /api/file/extract creates a temporary directory that is never cleaned up on success or failure. Repeated requests will exhaust disk space. Extracted files from potentially malicious archives persist indefinitely.

properties.remediation: Use tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as a context manager to ensure automatic cleanup, or explicitly call shutil.rmtree() in a finally block.
